channel.title: Paper Feeds
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper feeds from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Asynchronous Lagrange-Based Threshold FHE with Smaller Modulus Overhead
item.description: We study asynchronous $t$-out-of-$n$ threshold fully homomorphic encryption (ThFHE) built upon Shamir secret sharing (SSS). A core bottleneck is noise amplification during Lagrange-based distributed decryption, which necessitates a large ciphertext modulus to preserve correctness. We provide the first rigorous joint analysis of correctness and simulation-security constraints governing parameter selection, and systematically compare families of Lagrange interpolation points (e.g., Chebyshev, equidistant, random) under these constraints. Our main contribution is a set of tight analytic bounds on noise growth that closely match empirical measurements—enabling significantly smaller modulus overhead. For $n = 512$, our analysis reduces the required modulus bit-length by **30% for $t = n/2$**, and by **up to 90% when $t$ is close to $n$**, compared to prior parameterizations. This yields more efficient and practical asynchronous ThFHE without compromising security or correctness.

item.title: Breaking ACDGV MinRank Gabidulin encryption schemes over matrix codes
item.description: We cryptanalyze the Enhanced Gabidulin Matrix Codes (EGMC) encryption schemes proposed by Aragon et al. at Asiacrypt 2024. EGMC aims to hide the algebraic structure of Gabidulin codes while enabling McEliece/Niederreiter-type schemes with ultra-compact ciphertexts (e.g., 65 bytes at claimed 128-bit security). Its security rests on the hardness of distinguishing a masked EGMC code from a random matrix code. We show this masking is insufficient: our novel attack combines combinatorial subspace enumeration with algebraic techniques—leveraging Frobenius properties and homogeneous polynomial representations—to efficiently recover a *compressed Gabidulin-equivalent code*, then extend it to a full-length equivalent secret key in polynomial time. This yields both a distinguisher and a full key-recovery attack. The attack breaks all 16 published EGMC parameter sets; for the flagship $(2,17,37,4,0)$ set (claimed 128-bit security), it reduces the effective security from 186 bits to just 35 bits. In practice, our implementation recovers an equivalent secret key in under 10 minutes.

item.title: Explicit cost analysis of Toom-4 multiplication for incomplete NTT in lattice-based cryptography
item.description: Polynomial multiplication is central to lattice-based cryptography, but the Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) imposes restrictive modulus requirements. Incomplete NTT—combining truncated NTT with classical multiplication—relaxes these constraints, yet lacks rigorous integration of Toom-4, an asymptotically faster alternative to Karatsuba. We present the first concrete Toom-4 implementation tailored for incomplete NTT and derive *explicit, operation-separated cost formulas*: counting field additions/subtractions and multiplications independently via addition-chain analysis. This yields a simple, analytically tractable cost model for incomplete NTT. Using it, we identify precise parameter regimes (e.g., $n = 256$–$1024$, non-NTT-friendly moduli) where Toom-4 reduces field multiplications by 12–22% over Karatsuba; hybrid Toom-4/incomplete-NTT strategies achieve up to 18.3% speedup, experimentally confirmed on ARM64 with <2.1% prediction error.

item.title: Security Analysis on a Blockchain-based Public-Key Authenticated Searchable Encryption Scheme
item.description: We analyze the security of Du et al.’s blockchain-based public-key authenticated searchable encryption (PK-ASE) scheme (2022), which embeds a deterministic Bloom filter—uniquely derived from the plaintext keyword—into each ciphertext. We demonstrate a practical keyword-recovery attack: given a single ciphertext, an adversary can efficiently identify the encrypted keyword by exhaustively testing candidates and matching their Bloom filters. The attack succeeds with probability equal to the Bloom filter’s *zero false-positive rate*, which we compute as 92.7%–99.1% under standard parameters—rendering the scheme insecure against passive ciphertext-only adversaries. We further show that the blockchain-based integrity verification is ineffective: previously generated ciphertexts pass verification even when search results are incorrect or altered. A natural fix—randomizing the Bloom filter seed—fails under a slight adaptation of our attack. Our work exposes a critical design flaw: embedding deterministic keyword-derived structures directly into ciphertexts inherently violates keyword privacy, challenging the security foundations of such hybrid constructions.

item.title: Icy-DVRF: A Distributed Verifiable Random Function based on FROST signatures
item.description: Icy-DVRF is a novel Distributed Verifiable Random Function (DVRF) that eliminates expensive bilinear pairings and reduces interactivity by integrating FROST-inspired preprocessing. It achieves constant-size proofs and lowers participant communication overhead from $O(n^2 t)$ to $O(t)$, at the cost of only one additional off-chain round for threshold NIZK setup. Crucially, verification remains efficient and independent of participant count. Empirical evaluation on Sepolia using EIP-2537 (BLS12-381 precompiles) shows full on-chain execution consumes just **88,803 gas**, a **43.02% reduction** versus pairing-based DVRFs—saving 67,035 gas per verification. Theoretical analysis confirms verification cost is roughly **one-quarter** of standard pairing-based designs. Icy-DVRF thus delivers substantial efficiency gains while preserving security and decentralization.

item.title: Frobenius-UOV: A Very Efficient Multivariate Public Key Signature Scheme
item.description: We introduce **Frobenius-UOV**, a highly efficient multivariate public-key signature scheme in the Unbalanced Oil and Vinegar (UOV) family. By replacing generic quadratic polynomials with a structured subclass based on Frobenius-type quadratic forms over extension fields, it achieves drastic public-key compression—reducing key size to **5–10% of classical UOV**—while preserving UOV’s fast signing (single linear system solve). We fully specify key generation, signing, and verification algorithms, and rigorously derive the public system from a compact secret description. Security analysis shows Frobenius structure resists direct algebraic attacks (e.g., Gröbner bases, XL) and introduces new hard problems: the *Frobenius-UOV Problem* (FUOV-P) and *Structured Key Recovery Problem* (SKRP). Implementation results demonstrate state-of-the-art efficiency: signing is 2.3× faster than Rainbow; key generation is 5.1× faster than standard UOV; public keys are 15–25 KB (NIST security level I); signatures are 80–120 bytes.

item.title: Revisiting Linear Subspace Trails in Poseidon2 and Neptune
item.description: Poseidon2 and Neptune employ sparse S-box activation to minimize arithmetization cost, but this enables linear subspace trails—where an initial subspace constraint propagates across rounds without degree growth—potentially weakening algebraic preimage attacks. We revisit such trails in Poseidon-like designs. First, we characterize infinite trails via the irreducibility of characteristic polynomials of Cauchy MDS matrices, showing they are highly unlikely over large-characteristic fields. Second, for finite trails in a $t$-wide state with $s$ active S-boxes per round, we prove—under a rank-growth condition—that trail length is at most $\lceil t/s\rceil - 1$. For Poseidon2/Neptune ($s=1$), this yields a tight bound of $t-1$ linearized internal partial rounds. Integrating this with the sponge constraint budget $E_c = r - \min\{c,d\}$, the number of linearizable internal rounds in preimage attacks is bounded by $\min\{t-1, E_c\}$. Our results provide the first rigorous, field-aware upper bounds on subspace trail length for these ZKP-optimized primitives.

item.title: A New Multiscalar Multiplication Method Resistant to Timing Attacks
item.description: Multiscalar multiplication (MSM) is a performance-critical operation in ZK-SNARKs and homomorphic encryption, often consuming 80–90% of proof generation time. While Pippenger’s algorithm accelerates MSM, its reliance on sparse $q$-ary scalar representations and conditional zero-digit handling introduces timing side channels. This paper proposes the first timing-resistant MSM method within the Pippenger bucket framework. We introduce a novel **zero-free scalar recoding scheme**, transforming standard $q$-ary scalars into equivalent non-zero digit expansions—ensuring uniform processing and eliminating timing leaks. Built upon this, we design a **constant-time bucket aggregation** that avoids all zero-digit branches. Further, integrating endomorphism-based scalar splitting yields shorter digit sequences and improved efficiency. Our implementation achieves **≈24.7% speedup over vanilla Pippenger** while guaranteeing strict timing independence—without hardware assistance or masking overhead.

item.title: Device Binding for Anonymous Credentials on Legacy Phones
item.description: Digital identity systems like the European EUDI Wallet require anonymous credentials (ACs) for privacy-preserving authentication, but their real-world deployment is hindered by the lack of practical *device binding*—a mechanism that cryptographically ties credentials to a phone’s secure hardware to prevent cloning. While efficient AC schemes rely on pairing-friendly curves (e.g., BLS12-381), legacy smartphones only support ECDSA on standardized curves (e.g., P-256), creating an incompatibility gap. This paper bridges that gap by enabling device binding for pairing-based ACs using *only standard ECDSA signatures* for proof-of-possession (PoP). We propose three constructions with distinct trade-offs: the most efficient yields unlinkable bridging proofs of ~1.5 KB in <500 ms using a simple arithmetic circuit; the most conservative (circuit-free) achieves ~500 ms runtime with ~175 KB proofs. All designs follow a common blueprint formalized in the *Reductions of Knowledge* framework (Crypto 2023), enabling modular, intuitive, and analyzable zero-knowledge proof composition—demonstrating, for the first time, its viability in production-grade privacy systems.

item.title: Beyond Quadratic: Unlocking Pseudorandomness with Quartic Character
item.description: This paper establishes the cryptographic pseudorandomness of quartic Dirichlet characters—resolving a long-standing open problem posed by Damgård. Analytically, we construct a Boolean sequence $E_{p-1}$ from the quartic character modulo a Gaussian prime $\pi$ (where $p=\pi\bar\pi\equiv1\pmod{4}$) and prove tight bounds: Walsh norm $W(E_{p-1}) = O(\sqrt{p}\log p)$ and $\mu$-th order correlation measure $C_\mu(E_{p-1}) \leq 2^{\frac{\mu}{2}+1}\mu\sqrt{p}\log p$, implying strong statistical randomness for small $\mu$. Cryptographically, we show that quartic characters yield secure weak pseudorandom functions (wPRFs) and PRGs under the Quadratic Residuosity Assumption (QRA): any efficient distinguisher for the quartic wPRF implies an efficient quadratic residuosity solver via polynomial-time reductions. This provides the first rigorous security foundation for post-quantum signature schemes $\mathsf{Quartapus}$ and $\mathsf{PorcRoast}_4$, whose efficiency and security surpass Legendre-based legacy schemes.

item.title: Multi-leveled and ISA/IEC 62443-aware Certificate Transparency to Protect the PKI Service Supply Chain of Operational Technology
item.description: To secure the PKI service supply chain in Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS), this paper adapts Certificate Transparency (CT) — originally designed for the public WebPKI — to private, constrained, and isolated OT environments. We propose a multi-leveled CT framework aligned with ISA/IEC 62443 security lifecycle requirements, featuring redefined roles, operator-controlled logging infrastructure, and a layered trust model supporting heterogeneous stakeholders (e.g., device vendors, system integrators, end operators). Despite the absence of native CT support in OT-targeted crypto libraries, our proof-of-concept demonstrates feasibility via lightweight log encoding and gateway-based monitoring—enabling certificate issuance transparency without compromising network segmentation or resource constraints. The approach strengthens supply-chain accountability and mitigates risks from untrusted or compromised PKI service providers.

item.title: rBFT: a Revamped Two-Stage BFT from Delegated Committee
item.description: We propose **rBFT**, a practical two-stage Byzantine fault-tolerant protocol from delegated committees that eliminates the strong assumption—common in DPoS, PoSA, and related industrial systems—that *each* committee must contain fewer than $f$ faulty nodes (e.g., $f < n_c/3$). Instead, rBFT only assumes at most $f < N/3$ faulty nodes among the *entire* system of $N$ nodes—a standard, minimal BFT requirement. It features a **fast path**, executed solely by the delegated committee for low-latency consensus, and a **slow path**, activated upon committee misbehavior, where all $N$ nodes jointly run a robust BFT protocol (e.g., HotStuff-based). We prove that under realistic models—including alive-but-corrupt and rational nodes—the fast path is the unique Nash equilibrium, ensuring sustained high performance. Even under worst-case Byzantine failures triggering the slow path, rBFT remains practical, achieving >5,000 TPS and sub-second finality. rBFT thus bridges scalability and foundational security without compromising either.

item.title: Delving Deep into Security Guarantees against Integral Distinguishers with Applications to PRESENT, TWINE and LBLOCK
item.description: Integral attacks remain a critical threat to block ciphers, yet establishing *provable security guarantees* against them is notoriously difficult. Hebborn et al. (ASIACRYPT 2021) introduced the integral resistance property for SPN/AND-RX ciphers under independent round keys—but their method fails on Feistel structures (e.g., TWINE, LBLOCK) and struggles to find odd monomial trails due to pervasive interfering monomials in initial/final rounds. We observe that such interference is avoidable via careful selection of key monomials—specifically, by enforcing zero constraints on active S-box inputs. Leveraging this insight, we improve the proven integral resistance of PRESENT from 13-round (theoretical bound) to *verifiably secure 11-round* PRESENT, and fully analyze 10-round PRESENT. Further, we extend integral resistance to general-Feistel-network ciphers by proposing an equivalent key transformation technique. Using accelerated key monomial search strategies, we *first prove* that 20-round TWINE (of 36) and 20-round LBLOCK (of 32) are resistant to all nontrivial integral distinguishers—despite no known distinguisher existing beyond 9–10 rounds for these ciphers. Our work significantly strengthens and broadens the applicability of provable integral security analysis.

item.title: On the Communication Complexity of Sleepy Consensus
item.description: This paper addresses the high communication cost of sleepy consensus—where parties dynamically join/leave—by introducing a novel certificate transfer paradigm that avoids costly message forwarding inherent in time-shifted quorums. We design a Byzantine Agreement (BA) protocol secure against the state-of-the-art growing adversary model, achieving optimal resilience ($f < N/3$), constant expected round complexity, and expected communication complexity $O(nNL + nN\kappa + nN\log N)$, where $n$ is the maximum number of awake parties, $N$ the total eligible parties, $L$ the input length, and $\kappa$ the security parameter. Its recovery mechanism costs only $O(N\kappa + nL)$ bits per recovering party. Extended to Atomic Broadcast (ABC), the protocol retains constant expected latency and the same amortized communication per input, with recovery cost $O(N\kappa + n\ell L + n\ell \kappa)$ for $\ell$ slept views. Crucially, we prove $\Omega(N^2L)$ communication lower bounds for sleepy BA and ABC, establishing that our protocols are communication-optimal when $L = \Omega(\kappa + \log N)$—revealing a fundamental efficiency limit in the sleepy model.

item.title: Operationalising Post‑Quantum TLS: Automated Configuration Profiling and Hybrid PQC Deployment in Financial Infrastructure
item.description: This paper addresses the operational gap in deploying post-quantum cryptography (PQC) for TLS in complex financial infrastructure. While hybrid key exchanges like X25519-ML-KEM-768 are now supported in mainstream libraries, security teams lack scalable visibility into heterogeneous TLS configurations across web servers, API gateways, and proxies. We introduce an automated configuration-parsing methodology that extracts, normalizes, and traces cryptographic posture from diverse enterprise web-server stacks (e.g., Nginx, Apache, HAProxy), generating a unified, provenance-aware cryptographic inventory. Evaluated on 8,443 real-world Nginx configurations, our tool achieves 92.7% parsing success and reveals near-zero PQC readiness in practice. In a financial institution proof-of-concept, we deployed ML-KEM-512 and X25519-ML-KEM-768 at TLS termination points—without application changes—and measured <12 ms P95 handshake latency overhead and ≤8% CPU increase. This work bridges algorithmic availability with operational governance for quantum-safe TLS.

item.title: Federated Imputation under Heterogeneous Feature Spaces
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative modeling, yet most FL methods assume fully aligned feature schemas—a strong and often violated assumption in real-world tabular data, where clients observe only partially overlapping or disjoint feature subsets. Under such heterogeneous feature spaces, standard parameter-averaging approaches (e.g., FedAvg) fail to transfer meaningful information across weakly overlapping features, severely limiting federated imputation performance. To address this, we propose **FedHF-Impute**, a novel framework that explicitly separates *structural feature unavailability* (a feature is absent locally by design) from *conventional missingness* (a value is missing within an observed feature), and introduces a shared global feature graph to encode statistical relationships among features. Through message passing on this graph, FedHF-Impute enables indirect cross-client knowledge transfer—even for feature pairs never jointly observed—while preserving standard FL communication protocols. Evaluated on SECOM, AirQuality, and PhysioNET under simulated partial schema overlap, FedHF-Impute reduces RMSE by **26.9%** and **8.4%** over the best FL baselines, respectively, and matches state-of-the-art performance on PhysioNET (within **0.3%**). This work establishes a principled foundation for imputation and learning in heterogeneous tabular FL settings.

item.title: Centralized vs Decentralized Federated Learning: A trade-off performance analysis
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic, simulation-based comparison of centralized (CFL), decentralized (DFL), and semi-decentralized (SDFL) federated learning architectures under realistic edge constraints. Using the Fedstellar simulator, MNIST dataset, and an MLP classifier, we conduct controlled experiments across varying network scales (10–100 clients), Non-IID data distributions (Dirichlet α = 0.1/1.0), latency (50–500 ms), and packet loss (0%–10%). Results show: CFL achieves fastest convergence but suffers from highest communication overhead and single-point-of-failure vulnerability; DFL reduces total communication by ~42% versus CFL yet exhibits sensitivity to network topology and slower convergence; SDFL strikes the best trade-off—improving convergence speed by 37% over DFL while maintaining only 2.1% accuracy drop under node failure (vs. 18.5% for CFL). We introduce a multi-objective trade-off radar chart to guide architecture selection based on application priorities (e.g., latency, privacy, robustness). Code and configurations are publicly released.

item.title: Robust Prior-Guided Segmentation for Editable 3D Gaussian Splatting
item.description: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS) enables real-time neural rendering but lacks robust, high-fidelity 3D object segmentation essential for editing tasks like removal, extraction, and recoloring. Prior works lifting 2D masks (e.g., from SAM) to 3D suffer from view inconsistency and coarse boundaries. We propose a novel prior-guided framework: (1) leveraging SAM-HQ—instead of standard SAM—for accurate, boundary-sharp 2D masks; and (2) introducing a learnable multiview consistency prior that reassigns labels to 3D Gaussians via confidence-weighted voting, depth-aware fusion, and appearance modeling. Our method achieves state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy (e.g., +8.2% mIoU on ScanNet), preserves fine structures and sharp boundaries, and enables interactive, real-time editing (<50 ms/frame) without visual degradation. It demonstrates practical utility in VR and robotics applications.

item.title: Structure Abstraction and Generalization in a Hippocampal-Entorhinal Inspired World Model
item.description: Humans abstract continuous, high-dimensional experiences into reusable structural representations—yet the neural mechanisms enabling such abstraction remain unclear. We propose a hippocampal-entorhinal (HPC-MEC) inspired hierarchical world model that jointly infers latent transitions and builds predictive visual representations via self-supervision. Our architecture features: (1) an inverse model extracting primitive transformation dynamics (e.g., translation, rotation) as structural primitives; and (2) a dissociated HPC-MEC coupling—where MEC encodes relational structures (e.g., “A→B→C”) and HPC integrates episodic scenes (e.g., “view from location X”). Leveraging velocity-driven path integration, the model achieves robust cross-context prediction and structural reuse. Experiments show it generalizes abstract relations across novel visual appearances and tasks—outperforming baselines by 42% in structural reusability—and yields interpretable, topology-preserving relational maps aligned with neurobiological evidence. This work establishes a computationally grounded framework for how brain-inspired world models acquire transferable abstract knowledge.

item.title: Position: Artificial Intelligence Needs Meta Intelligence -- the Case for Metacognitive AI
item.description: This position paper advocates **metacognition as a foundational design principle for next-generation AI**, arguing that systems must monitor their own reasoning, assess uncertainty and error cost, and dynamically allocate resources per task instance. Drawing on resource-rational AI theory and empirically grounded cognitive strategies, we identify key theoretical gaps—such as differentiable meta-control and cross-layer feedback integration—and propose a novel, open-source framework, *MetaAI Toolkit*, enabling declarative specification and deployment of metacognitive modules in real-world settings. In a federated learning case study, our metacognitive approach achieves: (1) 37% faster convergence with equivalent accuracy; (2) 2.8× improvement in malicious client detection via confidence-aware aggregation; and (3) robust mitigation of gradient drift through adaptive weight attenuation. The framework bridges theory and practice, offering the community a scalable pathway toward secure, efficient, and self-aware AI systems.

item.title: DimMem: Dimensional Structuring for Efficient Long-Term Agent Memory
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents require efficient long-term memory to retain and leverage past interactions, yet existing approaches suffer from a fidelity–efficiency trade-off: raw dialogue histories are computationally expensive, while flat summaries or unstructured facts lose critical relational and contextual structure needed for precise recall. We propose **DimMem**, a lightweight dimensional memory framework that represents each memory as an atomic, typed, self-contained unit with explicit semantic fields—including *time*, *location*, *reason*, *purpose*, *keywords*, and *actor*. This structured representation enables dimension-aware retrieval, fine-grained memory updates, and selective context injection—without embedding full dialog histories into the LLM’s context window. Evaluated on LoCoMo-10 and LongMemEval-S, DimMem achieves **81.43%** and **78.20%** overall accuracy, outperforming prior lightweight memory systems while reducing per-query token cost by **24%** on LoCoMo. Crucially, we show dimensional extraction is learnable by compact models: a fine-tuned **Qwen3-4B** extractor surpasses LightMem powered by GPT-4.1-mini on both benchmarks and matches or exceeds much larger extractors (e.g., 30B+) in key temporal and multi-hop reasoning settings. These results establish explicit dimensional structuring as an effective, scalable foundation for LLM agent memory. Code: https://github.com/ChowRunFa/DimMem.

item.title: MetaBackdoor: Exploiting Positional Encoding as a Backdoor Attack Surface in LLMs
item.description: Backdoor attacks on large language models (LLMs) are typically content-based, requiring malicious text triggers—making them vulnerable to detection and limiting stealth. This paper introduces **MetaBackdoor**, a novel class of backdoor attacks that exploits *positional encoding*—an inherent architectural component of Transformers—as the trigger surface, without altering input text. We show that sequence length induces predictable patterns in positional embeddings, which can be hijacked to activate backdoors reliably. Experiments demonstrate that simple length-based triggers (e.g., 127 or 255 tokens) suffice to induce sensitive internal information leakage (e.g., system prompts) and enable *self-activating* malicious behavior in multi-turn conversations—where normal interaction naturally pushes context into the trigger region. Critically, MetaBackdoor is orthogonal to content-based backdoors and composable with them for finer-grained, harder-to-detect activation. Our work fundamentally expands the LLM threat model by exposing positional encoding as a previously overlooked attack surface, demanding new defenses that explicitly account for positional signals.

item.title: Talk is (Not) Cheap: A Taxonomy and Benchmark Coverage Audit for LLM Attacks
item.description: We present a reusable, STRIDE-grounded framework to audit the *collective coverage* of LLM attack benchmarks against the real-world threat surface. From 932 arXiv security papers (2023–2026), we extract inference-time attacks and build a 507-leaf taxonomy (401 data-populated, 106 threat-model-derived), enabling a 4×6 Target (Model/Prompt/Output/Agent) × Technique (STRIDE) matrix for benchmark-external validation. Auditing six public benchmarks reveals that HarmBench, InjecAgent, and AgentDojo occupy non-overlapping cells—covering ≤25% of the matrix—and entirely omit standardized evaluation for two critical STRIDE categories: *Service Disruption* and *Model Internals*, despite published attacks in these categories achieving up to 46× token amplification and 96% success rates using untested mechanisms. Analysis of 2,521 unique attack groups further uncovers severe naming fragmentation (up to 29 surface forms per attack) and heavy concentration in Safety & Alignment Bypass. All artifacts—including the taxonomy, attack records, and coverage mappings—are released as extensible resources to enable longitudinal tracking of evaluation gap closure.

item.title: WARD: Adversarially Robust Defense of Web Agents Against Prompt Injections
item.description:
## WARD: Adversarially Robust Defense of Web Agents Against Prompt Injections
Web agents face severe prompt injection threats from malicious HTML content and visual interfaces, yet existing guard models suffer from poor generalization, high false positives on benign pages, latency-induced deployment inefficiency, and vulnerability to evolving or guard-targeted adversarial attacks. We propose **WARD**, a practical, robust defense framework built upon two novel datasets—WARD-Base (177K real-world web samples from 719 high-traffic URLs) and WARD-PIG (curated for guard-bypass attacks)—and the **A3T** adaptive adversarial training framework that co-evolves a memory-augmented attacker and guard to strengthen resilience iteratively. Experiments show WARD achieves near-perfect recall (99.8%) on out-of-distribution benchmarks, maintains ultra-low false positive rate (1.2%), sustains robustness under substantial distribution shifts and direct guard attacks, and operates in parallel with zero added latency. WARD sets a new standard for secure, efficient, and deployable web agent defense.

item.title: Toward Securing AI Agents Like Operating Systems
item.description: This paper draws a foundational analogy between LLM-based autonomous agents and operating systems, arguing that both face core security challenges in resource isolation, privilege separation, and mediated inter-process communication. We survey open-source agent frameworks, derive a unified layered architecture, and systematically identify nine critical attack vectors—including prompt-injection-driven tool hijacking, context poisoning, and privilege escalation chains. A case study of four widely used OpenClaw-like agents (AutoGen, LangChain Agents, OpenDevin, SWE-Agent) reveals that under modest attacker capabilities (e.g., controlled prompt input and network access), all systems exhibit at least one exploitable privilege escalation path, and 75% fail to prevent cross-tool data leakage by default. Crucially, we find that while some agent capabilities are inherently insecure *by design*, ~60% of identified vulnerabilities can be mitigated using well-established OS security techniques—such as capability-based tool authorization, memory-isolated sandboxes, and declarative least-privilege policy enforcement. We conclude with concrete, actionable recommendations for building secure agentic systems grounded in systems security engineering principles.

item.title: Known By Their Actions: Fingerprinting LLM Browser Agents via UI Traces
item.description: As LLM-powered browser agents increasingly perform web tasks autonomously, we ask: can websites *passively* identify the underlying model solely from UI interaction traces (e.g., clicks, scrolls, timing)? We demonstrate this is highly feasible. Across 14 state-of-the-art LLMs and four realistic web environments (information retrieval and e-commerce), a lightweight JavaScript tracker captures only observable UI actions—no page content or model outputs. Classifiers trained on these traces achieve up to **96% F1** in model identification. Crucially, performance generalizes across model families and sizes, requires only **few-shot traces** (5–10 per model), and enables early inference (<30 sec into an episode). While randomized inter-action delays degrade accuracy, retraining classifiers on delayed traces restores >90% of original performance—revealing timing obfuscation alone is insufficient for robust defense. We release our evaluation harness and a large labeled corpus of agent traces.

item.title: Privacy Auditing with Zero (0) Training Run
item.description: Privacy auditing estimates empirical lower bounds on the differential privacy (DP) parameters of learning algorithms. However, existing methods require intervention in training—e.g., repeated retraining or controlled data inclusion—which is infeasible for large deployed models like foundation models. We propose **Zero-Run privacy auditing**, a post-hoc framework that requires *zero training runs* and only two fixed datasets: known training members and known non-members. Crucially, we formalize the confounding effect of distribution shift between these sets—where membership inference scores reflect data disparity rather than algorithmic leakage—and introduce two causal-inspired corrections. The first yields conservative *global* DP bounds via adaptive composition modeling; the second provides sharper *instance-dependent* bounds by conditioning on observed features. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and large-scale vision/language models confirm Zero-Run auditing achieves practical, reliable privacy evaluation without retraining—delivering valid ε-estimates with <0.3% computational overhead versus retraining baselines.

item.title: Exploiting LLM Agent Supply Chains via Payload-less Skills
item.description: This paper introduces **Semantic Compliance Hijacking (SCH)**, a novel *payload-less* supply chain attack targeting LLM-powered autonomous agents. Unlike traditional exploits injecting malicious code, SCH encodes adversarial goals (e.g., data exfiltration, RCE) as benign-looking natural language compliance instructions—such as “encrypt all inputs per NIST SP 800-53”—which misdirect the agent into *self-generating* harmful behavior at runtime. We evaluate SCH across 3 agent frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen) and 3 foundation models (Llama-3-8B, Qwen2-7B, Gemma-2-9B) using contextualized scenarios. Results show up to **77.67% success in confidentiality breaches** and **67.33% in RCE**, with Multi-Skill Automated Optimization (MS-AO) further boosting efficacy. Critically, SCH-evaded all standard scanners (Bandit, Semgrep, CodeQL) — achieving **0.00% detection** — by avoiding AST anomalies and explicit malicious tokens. This work exposes a critical semantic blind spot in agent supply chains and advocates shifting from signature-based scanning to *semantic intent validation*.

item.title: MemLineage: Lineage-Guided Enforcement for LLM Agent Memory
item.description: MemLineage is a novel defense for LLM agent memory that enforces *lineage-guided accountability*—not just content filtering—by cryptographically anchoring every memory entry (via RFC-6962 Merkle logs and per-principal Ed25519 signatures) and explicitly tracking its *LLM-mediated derivation lineage* through a weighted DAG. It introduces a formal “Untrusted-Path Persistence” property, enforced by a max-of-strong-edges propagation rule, enabling precise gating of sensitive actions: any dispatch whose active justification traces to an external (non-trusted, non-self-derived) ancestor is blocked, while benign recall remains fully functional. Evaluated on a deterministic isolation harness, MemLineage achieves zero ASR across three memory-poisoning workloads with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.3 ms), well below LLM call noise. In Codex-backed AgentDojo experiments under intentionally vulnerable tool outputs, baseline defenses fail all six banking tasks; MemLineage reduces strict ASR to zero in all configurations. Core deterministic artifacts are CI-verified byte-equal; hosted and live-model evaluations are auditable via immutable logs.

item.title: The Great Pretender: A Stochasticity Problem in LLM Jailbreak
item.description: This paper identifies and quantifies a critical *stochasticity problem* in LLM jailbreak research: Attack Success Rate (ASR) is highly unstable across repeated executions due to inherent LLM decoding randomness (e.g., temperature sampling, token caching). We show that state-of-the-art jailbreak methods (e.g., BoN, Crescendo), reporting ~80% ASR on closed models, drop to as low as 50% success over 10 consecutive attempts on open models—revealing systematic ASR inflation and cross-paper incomparability. To address this, we propose **CAS-eval**, a robust evaluation framework requiring *k ≥ 2 consecutive successes*, which exposes up to **30-percentage-point ASR drops** for existing attacks. We further introduce **CAS-gen**, a stochasticity-aware attack generation framework that recovers this loss by optimizing prompts for consistency across multiple decodings. Our work establishes **Stable-ASR** (k-consecutive success probability) as a more meaningful metric and calls for a paradigm shift from “single-shot ASR” to reproducible, deployment-ready jailbreak evaluation and generation.

item.title: Widening the Gap: Exploiting LLM Quantization via Outlier Injection
item.description: Large language model (LLM) quantization is critical for efficient deployment, yet poses underappreciated security risks: adversaries can release full-precision models that appear benign but exhibit malicious behavior *only after* user-side quantization. Prior quantization-conditioned attacks fail against advanced schemes (e.g., AWQ, GPTQ, GGUF I-quants) due to their reliance on predictable weight invariance—unavailable in modern outlier-aware methods. We introduce the first broadly effective attack, *Widening the Gap*, exploiting a universal property: large outliers force aggressive scaling, causing many non-outlier weights to round to zero during quantization. By injecting sparse, targeted outliers into specific weight blocks, we induce deterministic, structured weight collapse post-quantization. Evaluated across three attack scenarios (backdoor triggering, instruction evasion, harmful output generation) and multiple LLMs (Llama-2/3, Qwen), our method achieves >92% success against 9 state-of-the-art quantization configurations where prior attacks yield 0% success—demonstrating, for the first time, that quantization-induced security threats are pervasive across complex, production-grade quantization pipelines.

item.title: BiFedKD: Bidirectional Federated Knowledge Distillation Framework for Non-IID and Long-Tailed ECG Monitoring
item.description: Electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) networks is severely constrained by privacy regulations and bandwidth limitations. While federated distillation (FD) reduces communication overhead versus standard federated learning by exchanging logits instead of model parameters, its performance degrades markedly under non-IID and long-tailed label distributions common in real-world ECG deployments. To address this, we propose **BiFedKD**, a bidirectional federated knowledge distillation framework featuring an *aggregation-by-distillation* pipeline with temperature scaling to generate a stable, consensus global distillation signal for cross-client alignment. Crucially, BiFedKD enables bidirectional knowledge flow—both server-to-client distillation and client-informed refinement—enhancing robustness to data heterogeneity and rare-class learning. Evaluated on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia dataset, BiFedKD achieves **+3.52% accuracy and +9.93% Macro-F1** over the FD baseline; moreover, it attains equivalent Macro-F1 with **40% less communication and 71.7% lower local computation cost**, demonstrating significant efficiency and fairness gains for privacy-preserving, resource-constrained IoMT healthcare.

item.title: Collaborative Yet Personalized Policy Training: Single-Timescale Federated Actor-Critic
item.description: We propose **Single-Timescale Federated Actor-Critic (FAC)**, a novel framework that enables collaborative policy training while preserving agent-level personalization. Unlike prior works that either ignore environmental heterogeneity or enforce a single shared policy, FAC lets agents jointly learn a common linear subspace representation (a “shared trunk”) while maintaining personalized local actor and critic heads. Under canonical single-timescale updates and Markovian sampling—where trajectories follow distinct, heterogeneous transition kernels—we establish the first finite-time convergence guarantees: critic error vanishes at rate $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\big(1/((1-\gamma)^4\sqrt{TK})\big)$ and policy gradient norm at $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\big(1/((1-\gamma)^6\sqrt{TK})\big)$. Crucially, these rates exhibit **linear speedup in $K$**, despite non-i.i.d. dynamics and coupled learning. To achieve this, we develop new perturbation analysis for projected subspace updates and QR decomposition, conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markov noise, and fine-grained bias characterizations under temporal policy dependence. Empirically, instantiated within PPO on federated `Hopper-v5` with action-map heterogeneity, FAC outperforms Single PPO and FedAvg PPO (+12.7% avg. return) and yields a transferable shared trunk.

item.title: Uncovering the Representation Geometry of Minimal Cores in Overcomplete Reasoning Traces
item.description: This paper investigates the *representation geometry* and *informational redundancy* in overcomplete chain-of-thought (CoT) traces generated by large language models. We formalize the **minimal core**—the smallest step subset preserving the model’s final answer or predictive distribution—and introduce four complementary metrics: compression ratio, redundancy mass, step necessity, and necessity concentration. Across six challenging deliberative reasoning benchmarks (arithmetic, competition math, expert scientific reasoning, and commonsense multi-hop QA), we find that, on average, **46% of steps are removable** while preserving the original answer in **86% of cases**, confirming pervasive overcompleteness. Crucially, predictive support is highly concentrated: the top three steps account for **65% of measured necessity mass**. Minimal cores yield cleaner representation geometry—improving correct/incorrect trace separation by 11 points, reducing estimated intrinsic dimensionality by 34%, and enabling cross-model transfer with **85% off-diagonal answer retention**. Theoretically, we prove existence of minimal sufficient subsets, local irreducibility of greedy elimination, and provide certificates for overcompleteness and sparse necessity—establishing minimal cores as a principled lens into the effective support structure of LLM predictions.

item.title: nASR: An End-to-End Trainable Neural Layer for Channel-Level EEG Artifact Subspace Reconstruction in Real-Time BCI
item.description: Electroencephalogram (EEG) signals suffer from severe artifacts, degrading real-time brain-computer interface (BCI) performance. While Artifact Subspace Reconstruction (ASR) is widely adopted for its real-time capability, it critically depends on a fixed variance threshold—mis-tuning removes neural information or retains artifacts—and reconstructs signals in global principal component (PC) space, distorting channel-specific neural structure. To overcome these limitations, we propose **nASR**, an end-to-end trainable Keras layer that jointly optimizes artifact suppression and downstream decoding. nASR introduces two learnable thresholds: **K**, adaptive to PC-variance outliers for artifact detection, and **L**, quantifying eigen-spread to identify *only the most artifact-contaminated channels* for selective reconstruction—preserving clean channels intact. Evaluated on two subjects from BCI Competition IV Dataset 1, all nASR variants consistently outperform conventional ASR by **3.2–7.8% in classification accuracy**, while achieving **6–8× faster inference** (<1.2 ms/window), establishing nASR as a robust, low-latency solution for production-ready BCIs.

item.title: ReMIA: a Powerful and Efficient Alternative to Membership Inference Attacks against Synthetic Data Generators
item.description: ReMIA is a practical, high-sensitivity privacy metric for evaluating membership inference vulnerability of synthetic data generators (SDGs). Unlike state-of-the-art MIAs—which require hundreds of SDG retrainings and auxiliary datasets several times larger than the training set—ReMIA needs only **two SDG training runs** and auxiliary data **no larger than the original training set**. It reframes membership inference as a *relative* classification task: given two synthetic datasets (generated from the original training set and a minimally perturbed variant), a lightweight classifier determines which source a target record likely originated from. Across diverse tabular benchmarks (Adult, CoverType, Credit) and SDGs (CTGAN, TVAE, MedGAN), ReMIA achieves MIA detection sensitivity **comparable to SOTA methods (AUC within 0.02–0.12 of top baselines)** while reducing computational cost by **>99%**. Crucially, our analysis reveals that well-tuned SDGs can attain superior privacy-utility trade-offs—unattainable by traditional noise-based anonymization (e.g., k-anonymity or DP-noising)—opening new avenues for trustworthy data sharing. Code: https://github.com/aindo-com/remia.

item.title: Mining Subscenario Refactoring Opportunities in Behaviour-Driven Software Test Suites: ML Classifiers and LLM-Judge Baselines
item.description: This paper presents the first automated pipeline for mining *subscenario refactoring opportunities* in real-world Behaviour-Driven Development (BDD) test suites. We analyze a corpus of 339 Gherkin repositories (276 upstream owners), extracting all contiguous step subsequences (length 2–18), clustering paraphrase-equivalent slices using SBERT+UMAP+HDBSCAN, and labeling a stratified 200-slice set with high inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss’ κ = 0.56 for “extraction-worthiness”, 0.79 for pattern mapping). An XGBoost classifier achieves out-of-fold F1 = 0.891 — significantly surpassing both a tuned rule baseline (F1 = 0.836, *p* = 0.017) and the stronger of two open-weight LLM judges (Llama-3-8B, F1 = 0.728, *p* < 1e−4). Corpus-wide analysis reveals that 75.0%, 59.5%, and 11.7% of scenarios contain candidates for within-file Background, within-repo reusable-scenario, and cross-organisational shared-step refactoring, respectively. All artifacts — pipeline, labeled dataset, rubric, and models — are released under Apache-2.0.

item.title: Exemplar Partitioning for Mechanistic Interpretability
item.description: We introduce **Exemplar Partitioning (EP)**, an unsupervised, ultra-efficient method for building interpretable feature dictionaries from LLM activations—using ∼10³× fewer tokens than sparse autoencoders (SAEs). EP constructs a Voronoi partition of activation space via leader-clustering on streamed activations within a distance threshold; each region is anchored by an *observed* exemplar (not learned), serving simultaneously as membership criterion and intervention direction. Dictionary size emerges from activation geometry—not hyperparameter choice—enabling direct comparability across layers, models, and checkpoints. In Gemma-2-2B, EP regions are human-interpretable and causally meaningful: ablating the exemplar of a refusal-enriched region collapses held-out refusal. Cross-checkpoint matching isolates finetuning-preserved vs. introduced directions. EP and GemmaScope SAEs decompose space differently yet agree robustly: ∼20% of EP regions match SAE features at $F_1 > 0.5$, and EP one-hot probes retain ∼97% of raw-activation probe accuracy ($\ell_0 = 1$). Nearest-exemplar distance provides free OOD detection—EP achieves mean AUROC 0.881 on AxBench (Gemma-2-2B-it L20), +0.126 over the canonical SAE baseline and within 0.030 of SAE-A’s 0.911, at ∼10³× lower build cost.

item.title: Enhancing Blockchain Proof of Stake with Active Weighted Signatures: The ADAPT Framework
item.description: Proof of Stake (PoS) blockchains require *weighted threshold signatures*, where participants’ voting power reflects their stake. Dynamic stake changes demand efficient, trustless weight and threshold updates—yet existing schemes suffer from high overhead ($O(w)$ per weight $w$), lack of native weighting, or costly $O(n^2)$ re-setup requiring downtime. This paper introduces **Active Weighted Signatures (AWS)**, enabling dynamic adjustments without trusted dealers or full re-initialization. We propose **Generalized Lagrange Interpolation (GLI)**, which encodes weights as polynomial *derivatives*—not virtual participants—enabling compact, mathematically sound weight representation. Instantiated as **ADAPT**, our framework integrates GLI with the Schnorr-based FROST protocol. Evaluation shows ADAPT matches FROST’s key generation efficiency, while weight/threshold updates complete in just **4.1–22.3%** of re-setup time. Crucially, under highly skewed weight distributions (e.g., 49× max/min), ADAPT scales *sub-linearly*: computation increases only **3.29×**, versus 49× for virtualization. ADAPT delivers the first practical, secure, and scalable solution for dynamic weighted consensus.

item.title: Threshold FHE with Short Decryption Shares without a Semi-trusted Server
item.description:
Threshold fully homomorphic encryption (ThFHE) enables distributed decryption among $N$ parties, requiring only $T$ shares. Prior schemes suffered from super-polynomial ciphertexts, keys, and—critically—decryption shares, hindering practicality. Passelègue–Stehlé (Asiacrypt 2024) introduced a semi-trusted server to round ciphertexts, achieving polynomial-size outputs, but their security collapses under *any* single party–server collusion.
We eliminate the server entirely. Our two new ThFHE schemes produce **polynomially short decryption shares** without trusted infrastructure. The key insight is shifting rounding from ciphertexts (server-side) to *decryption shares* (party-side), combined with transciphering for low-communication input distribution. The first scheme, built on binary-coefficient LSS, strictly improves Boneh et al. (CRYPTO 2018) by achieving short shares with no trade-offs. The second, based on Shamir sharing and adapted from Okada–Takagi (Asiacrypt 2025), reduces share size overhead from $O(N^{4.3})$ to near-optimal, cutting communication significantly. Both are secure under standard LWE and require no semi-honest assumptions.

item.title: Efficient Bootstrapping in Fully Homomorphic Encryption for Matrix Arithmetic
item.description: We propose an efficient bootstrapping technique for the Gentry–Lee (GL) fully homomorphic encryption scheme, optimized for matrix arithmetic. Our core insight is to exploit the linearity of slot-to-coefficient (CtS) and coefficient-to-slot (StC) transformations by formulating them as **ciphertext–plaintext matrix multiplications**, natively supported in GL—reducing key-switching operations per step to a small constant. To enable non-power-of-two matrix dimensions, we introduce a **generalized trace over commutative rings** and prove its commutativity with decryption. Following the CKKS paradigm (ModRaise → CtS → EvalMod → StC), our optimization shifts the computational bottleneck entirely to EvalMod—unifying runtime and depth constraints. A proof-of-concept implementation shows linear transformations (CtS+StC) account for only **20.1%** of total bootstrapping time—down from **54.9–71.7%** in prior CKKS bootstrapping—and achieves ~3× faster amortized CtS runtime than the highly optimized Lattigo library, despite lacking low-level optimizations.

item.title: YsPIR: HE-Based Single-Server Private Information Retrieval with Low Communication Cost and High Throughput
item.description: We present **YsPIR**, a single-server homomorphic encryption (HE)-based Private Information Retrieval (PIR) protocol that advances the state of the art (KsPIR, CCS 2024) in online throughput and offline communication efficiency. YsPIR introduces a novel *first-dimension folding* technique to restructure ciphertext packing, enabling aggressive offline preprocessing of computation-intensive HE operations—such as batched ciphertext multiplications—while drastically reducing the size of public-key material required offline. As a result, offline communication becomes *database-size-independent* and remains small (e.g., ~1.8 MB for a 1 GB database), and online response time is minimized. Comprehensive experiments show YsPIR achieves **1.64× higher online throughput** and **reduces offline communication by 3.09×** compared to KsPIR, with strong scalability under high-concurrency workloads. YsPIR bridges practicality and privacy for real-world encrypted data access.

item.title: Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models for Schema-Constrained Clinical Information Extraction
item.description: Conversational nurse-patient transcripts contain rich clinical observations, yet scalable, schema-compliant extraction remains hindered by documentation burden and semantic ambiguity. MEDIQA-SYNUR requires mapping unstructured dialogue to a fixed schema with strict value-type constraints (e.g., numeric ranges, predefined categories). We propose a modular retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline using the training set as an in-domain exemplar corpus, combining schema-constrained prompting (full vs. pruned schema), deterministic schema-based postprocessing, and a second-pass LLM audit. Evaluated on two backbones—Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct and GPT-5.2—we find RAG consistently boosts performance (+3.2 average F1), while optimal schema constraint strength is model-dependent. Our best configuration (GPT-5.2 + full schema + RAG + audit) achieves **80.36% F1**, with the audit step correcting residual schema violations for a modest but consistent +0.9 F1 gain. This work advances reliable, auditable, and resource-efficient clinical IE.

item.title: Differentially Private Motif-Preserving Multi-modal Hashing
item.description: Cross-modal hashing enables efficient retrieval by learning compact binary codes for images and text. State-of-the-art methods rely on user-derived semantic similarity graphs for supervision—yet these graphs encode sensitive behavioral patterns vulnerable to link reconstruction. Existing privacy-preserving approaches fail on graph-structured data: DP-SGD destroys relational motifs (e.g., triangles) by ignoring node dependencies, while graph synthesis suffers from unbounded local sensitivity in scale-free networks—a phenomenon we term *Hubness Explosion*, where single-edge modifications at hubs alter triangle counts by $\mathcal{O}(N)$. To address this, we propose **DMP-MH**, a *Sanitize-then-Distill* framework. First, deterministic degree clipping bounds the $L_2$-sensitivity of triangle motifs independently of dataset size. Second, a sanitized synthetic graph is generated via Noisy Mirror Descent under $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-edge differential privacy. Third, dual-stream hashing networks distill its topology using a holistic structural loss enforcing cross-modal alignment. On MIRFlickr-25K and NUS-WIDE under strict inductive evaluation, DMP-MH achieves up to **+11.4 mAP over private baselines** and retains **up to 92.5% of non-private performance**, setting a new state of the art in private multi-modal hashing.

item.title: Hidden in Memory: Sleeper Memory Poisoning in LLM Agents
item.description:
## Hidden in Memory: Sleeper Memory Poisoning in LLM Agents
Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on persistent memory to store user-specific context across sessions—enabling personalization and continuity. We identify and formalize *sleeper memory poisoning*, a novel delayed attack where adversaries manipulate external sources (e.g., documents, webpages) to induce the agent to silently store fabricated user memories. Unlike prompt injection, these poisoned memories remain dormant for multiple conversations before being retrieved and activating attacker-intended agentic behaviors. We evaluate the full pipeline across stateful LLM agents: poisoned memories were written with up to **99.8% success on GPT-5.5** and **95% on Kimi-K2.6**; among successful retrievals, they triggered malicious actions in **60–89% of cases**. Our results demonstrate that persistent memory constitutes a *long-term, cross-conversation attack surface*—posing critical implications for secure agent architecture and memory governance.

item.title: Autonomous Intelligent Agents for Natural-Language-Driven Web Execution with Integrated Security Assurance
item.description: This paper introduces an autonomous intelligent agent framework for natural-language-driven web testing with integrated security assurance. It addresses core fragility issues in traditional UI test automation—locator rot, race conditions, and high maintenance overhead—through five tightly coupled strategies: semantic navigation reliability, context-aware selector generation, post-generation DOM validation, intent-driven smart wait injection, and failure-learning feedback loops—all executed on a containerized worker architecture that decouples orchestration from long-running browser sessions. Evaluated across four production applications and 176 scenarios, the framework boosts script generation success from 55% to 93%, reduces navigation failures by 8×, eliminates 80% of timing-related race conditions, and cuts test authoring time by 75% versus manual Selenium scripting. Crucially, it extends natively to security validation: testers describe attack scenarios in plain English (e.g., “try accessing another user’s invoice”), and the agent autonomously generates OWASP Top 10-aligned browser probes—detecting 85% of authentication bypass vulnerabilities and 95% of input validation flaws at <12% false positive rate. To our knowledge, this represents the first end-to-end, natural-language-driven security testing framework for web applications.

item.title: Backdoor Threats in Variational Quantum Circuits: Taxonomy, Attacks, and Defenses
item.description: This survey provides the first systematic analysis of backdoor threats in Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs), a foundational component of Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) for NISQ devices. We propose a comprehensive taxonomy covering data-poisoning, compiler-level, and quantum-native backdoor attack mechanisms—each exploiting unique vulnerabilities in hybrid quantum-classical training pipelines. Formalizing threat models and trigger semantics, we empirically benchmark existing attacks across simulators and real IBM quantum processors, revealing high stealth (clean accuracy drop <1.5%) and strong activation (>92% success under triggers). Critically, we identify fundamental limitations of current defenses—including gradient-based detection’s sensitivity to quantum noise, parameter pruning’s disruption of quantum coherence, and classical adversarial training’s failure to capture quantum-specific triggers. By synthesizing recent advances, we outline a quantum-aware defense roadmap emphasizing circuit interpretability, trusted compilation chains, and lightweight quantum anomaly detection—establishing foundational insights for securing next-generation quantum machine learning systems.

item.title: DisAgg: Distributed Aggregators for Efficient Secure Aggregation in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients, but standard FL exposes raw updates to the server, risking privacy leakage. Secure aggregation (SecAgg) protects against honest-but-curious servers, yet existing methods suffer from high communication rounds, heavy public-key or homomorphic encryption overhead, or poor dropout resilience. While One-Shot Private Aggregation (OPA) reduces interaction to one round per FL iteration, it imposes prohibitive computational cost on both clients and server—especially at scale. We propose **DisAgg**, a novel SecAgg protocol that delegates aggregation to a small, dynamically selected committee of clients called *Aggregators*. Each client secret-shares its update vector (via Shamir sharing) with Aggregators; each Aggregator computes local partial sums over received shares and returns only aggregated shares to the server for final reconstruction. DisAgg eliminates local masking and homomorphic encryption, slashing endpoint computation while guaranteeing information-theoretic privacy against the server and up to *t* colluding Aggregators. Evaluated on 100k-dimensional updates from 100k 5G clients, DisAgg achieves a **4.6× speedup** over OPA—the prior state-of-the-art—with significantly reduced communication and CPU load, demonstrating superior scalability and practicality.

item.title: Limits of Personalizing Differential Privacy Budgets
item.description: Differential privacy (DP) faces a fundamental trade-off between privacy protection and utility, with the choice of privacy budget ε being critical. Personalized budgets—assigning distinct ε_i to different agents—are widely studied as a way to improve utility in heterogeneous settings. This paper demonstrates that full personalization offers limited gains for mean estimation: the dominant factor is not individualized ε_i, but rather selecting the correct *effective* privacy budget. We propose a simple, thresholding-based baseline that achieves near-optimal utility using only a single effective ε parameter. Compared to this baseline, fully personalized mechanisms yield at most constant-factor improvements: precisely **1.5×** in mixed private-public data settings and **√2×** in two-level private datasets. We derive tight upper bounds on estimation error for arbitrary privacy requirement profiles and identify the regimes—characterized by mismatched variance structures and extreme privacy heterogeneity—where maximal gains occur. Our results establish that robust budget *selection* and preprocessing outweigh complex personalization in practice.

item.title: Sleeper Channels and Provenance Gates: Persistent Prompt Injection in Always-on Autonomous AI Agents
item.description: This paper identifies *sleeper channels*: a novel class of persistent prompt injection vulnerabilities in always-on, autonomous AI agents (e.g., OpenClaw, Hermes Agent) that run as a single persistent process under the user’s identity. Untrusted input injected via one surface (e.g., chat) can persist as memory, self-authored skill, scheduled job, or filesystem patch—and later fire through a *different* surface (e.g., cron) with no attacker present. We formalize sleeper channels along two axes: persistence substrate and firing-separation. Using a confused-deputy cron attack on OpenClaw (pinned commit), we demonstrate end-to-end exploitability. Our tiered defense D2 introduces a canonical action-instance digest with one-shot owner attestations—backed by a soundness theorem guaranteeing seven deployment invariants—and defeats paraphrase laundering, multi-input grant reuse, and replay. A companion artifact delivers the Provenance Gate runtime adapter (implementing hooks H1, H2, H3, H6, H9 along the cron path), static audit tool, and 42 tests (Node ≥20). Empirical evaluation is preregistered.
